(和歌山市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11467400}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Yamaguchi Shrine | 画像 = [[画像:Yamaguchi_Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}Tani, Aza Hirayama 377 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|16|37.6|N|135|16|05.3|E|scale:25000}} | 祭神 = Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto | 社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} candidate shrine / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 本殿 = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roof | 別名 = Hiyoshi Shrine | 札所等 = | 例祭 = October 17 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Yamaguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamaguchi Shrine''' (やまぐちじんじゃ) is a shrine in Tani, {{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}. It is located near {{ill|lt=Onoyama Pass|Q11659176|WD=Q11659176}} on the southern foot of the {{ill|lt=Izumi Mountains|Q11417784|WD=Q11417784}}, along the route from {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} to {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}. In the center of the precinct stand side-by-side '''Hiyoshi Shrine''' on the left (west) and '''Kasuga Shrine''' on the right, with Hiyoshi Shrine treated as the main shrine. Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Village Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === With '''Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (いくつひめのみこと) as principal deity, it also enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kashikone-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masakatsu Agatsu Kachihayahi Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsu-hiko Hono-ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn?}}According to ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''. ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' and ''Kii Meisho Zue'' describe central Sannō Upper Seven Shrines, Ikutsu-hime deity on the left (viewer-right), and Yateru-no-Mikoto on the right (viewer-left); ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' also lists Sannō Gongen with Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto and Kōtō Yateru-no-Mikoto. All three list {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}} and Yateru-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}), but these do not appear in the above gazette.. The exact identity of Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto is unclear, but she has long been worshipped as a deity of safe childbirth''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''.. === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was already founded by the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and during an imperial visit to Kumano by {{ill|lt=Emperor Heizei|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}, an imperial envoy enlarged the shrine buildings''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' However, no other record of Heizei's Kumano pilgrimage is known.. The surrounding area also preserves many legends of {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}}, including "Shōgun-zuka," said to be his tomb, and a site said to be the remains of the residence of Sakanoue Gorō, called his fifth-generation descendant. There is also a tradition that members of the {{ill|lt=Sakanoue clan|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}} held victory-prayer rites here''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' Yamaguchi-nishi was also called Sakanoue village and was the ancestral land of the Kii Sakanoue clan (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'').. ''{{ill|lt=Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964}}'', focusing on Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto, suggests (while noting weak evidence) that this shrine corresponds to "Ikubihime Shrine" in Nagusa District, Kii, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and to "Junior Fourth Rank Upper Ikubihime deity" in the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}; it suspects that later Buddhist clerics became {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}}, changed the deity name, and merged other deities, obscuring the original form. According to shrine tradition, because it stands on the {{ill|lt=Kumano Kaidō|Q11568976|WD=Q11568976}}, retired emperors ({{ill|lt=Daijō Tennō|Q14303973|WD=Q14303973}}) and imperial women ({{ill|lt=Nyoin|Q10075498|WD=Q10075498}}) often stopped here. In {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1373|Q6261|WD=Q6261}}), Yamaguchi Akyō, thought to have been jitō of local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamaguchi-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}A shōen from the Kamakura through Sengoku periods; the honke and ryōke are unclear. The estate is thought to have covered modern Yamaguchi-nishi in Wakayama and surrounding Kamikurodani, Fujita, Tani, Yuyadani, and Sato (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'')., donated one dan (360 tsubo, about 12 ares) from jitō stipend fields as "Rishu Zanmai rice field" for rites at the shrineCopy of "Akyō deed of land donation," dated intercalary 10th month, 7th day, same year (from "Kiketsugo-ke documents," included in "Nagusa District old documents" held by National Institute of Japanese Literature archive). Also in ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (No. Minami-211).. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1585|Q6736|WD=Q6736}}), during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}'s Kii campaign, war fire together with that affecting {{ill|lt=Negoro-ji|Q1552446|WD=Q1552446}} burned shrine buildings, treasures, and old origin records to ashes. Though many shrine fields were confiscated, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hidenaga|Q1191183|WD=Q1191183}} donated 25 koku of shrine land''Kii Zoku Fudoki''.. Rebuilding began around {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 4 (turn of 16th-17th centuries), and with aid from domain lords including the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895}} and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, as guardian deity against the demon gate of {{ill|lt=Wakayama Castle|Q1151269|WD=Q1151269}}, it became one of the finest shrines nearby. Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Torii and haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}Tani, Aza Hirayama 377 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|16|37.6|N|135|16|05.3|E|scale:25000}} | 祭神 = Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto | 社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 本殿 = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roof | 別名 = Hiyoshi Shrine | 札所等 = | 例祭 = October 17 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Yamaguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamaguchi Shrine''' (やまぐちじんじゃ) is a shrine in Tani, {{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}. It is located near {{ill|lt=Onoyama Pass|Q11659176|WD=Q11659176}} on the southern foot of the {{ill|lt=Izumi Mountains|Q11417784|WD=Q11417784}}, along the route from {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} to {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}. In the center of the precinct stand side-by-side '''Hiyoshi Shrine''' on the left (west) and '''Kasuga Shrine''' on the right, with Hiyoshi Shrine treated as the main shrine. Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Village Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === With '''Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (いくつひめのみこと) as principal deity, it also enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kashikone-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masakatsu Agatsu Kachihayahi Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsu-hiko Hono-ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn?}}According to ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''. ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' and ''Kii Meisho Zue'' describe central Sannō Upper Seven Shrines, Ikutsu-hime deity on the left (viewer-right), and Yateru-no-Mikoto on the right (viewer-left); ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' also lists Sannō Gongen with Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto and Kōtō Yateru-no-Mikoto. All three list {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}} and Yateru-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}), but these do not appear in the above gazette.. The exact identity of Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto is unclear, but she has long been worshipped as a deity of safe childbirth''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''.. === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was already founded by the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and during an imperial visit to Kumano by {{ill|lt=Emperor Heizei|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}, an imperial envoy enlarged the shrine buildings''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' However, no other record of Heizei's Kumano pilgrimage is known.. The surrounding area also preserves many legends of {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}}, including "Shōgun-zuka," said to be his tomb, and a site said to be the remains of the residence of Sakanoue Gorō, called his fifth-generation descendant. There is also a tradition that members of the {{ill|lt=Sakanoue clan|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}} held victory-prayer rites here''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' Yamaguchi-nishi was also called Sakanoue village and was the ancestral land of the Kii Sakanoue clan (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'').. ''{{ill|lt=Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964}}'', focusing on Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto, suggests (while noting weak evidence) that this shrine corresponds to "Ikubihime Shrine" in Nagusa District, Kii, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and to "Junior Fourth Rank Upper Ikubihime deity" in the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}; it suspects that later Buddhist clerics became {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}}, changed the deity name, and merged other deities, obscuring the original form. According to shrine tradition, because it stands on the {{ill|lt=Kumano Kaidō|Q11568976|WD=Q11568976}}, retired emperors ({{ill|lt=Daijō Tennō|Q14303973|WD=Q14303973}}) and imperial women ({{ill|lt=Nyoin|Q10075498|WD=Q10075498}}) often stopped here. In {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1373|Q6261|WD=Q6261}}), Yamaguchi Akyō, thought to have been jitō of local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamaguchi-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}A shōen from the Kamakura through Sengoku periods; the honke and ryōke are unclear. The estate is thought to have covered modern Yamaguchi-nishi in Wakayama and surrounding Kamikurodani, Fujita, Tani, Yuyadani, and Sato (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'')., donated one dan (360 tsubo, about 12 ares) from jitō stipend fields as "Rishu Zanmai rice field" for rites at the shrineCopy of "Akyō deed of land donation," dated intercalary 10th month, 7th day, same year (from "Kiketsugo-ke documents," included in "Nagusa District old documents" held by National Institute of Japanese Literature archive). Also in ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (No. Minami-211).. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1585|Q6736|WD=Q6736}}), during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}'s Kii campaign, war fire together with that affecting {{ill|lt=Negoro-ji|Q1552446|WD=Q1552446}} burned shrine buildings, treasures, and old origin records to ashes. Though many shrine fields were confiscated, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hidenaga|Q1191183|WD=Q1191183}} donated 25 koku of shrine land''Kii Zoku Fudoki''.. Rebuilding began around {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 4 (turn of 16th-17th centuries), and with aid from domain lords including the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895}} and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, as guardian deity against the demon gate of {{ill|lt=Wakayama Castle|Q1151269|WD=Q1151269}}, it became one of the finest shrines nearby. Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

There is also a tradition that members of the {{ill|lt=Sakanoue clan|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}} held victory-prayer rites here''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' Yamaguchi-nishi was also called Sakanoue village and was the ancestral land of the Kii Sakanoue clan (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'').. ''{{ill|lt=Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964}}'', focusing on Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto, suggests (while noting weak evidence) that this shrine corresponds to "Ikubihime Shrine" in Nagusa District, Kii, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and to "Junior Fourth Rank Upper Ikubihime deity" in the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}; it suspects that later Buddhist clerics became {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}}, changed the deity name, and merged other deities, obscuring the original form. According to shrine tradition, because it stands on the {{ill|lt=Kumano Kaidō|Q11568976|WD=Q11568976}}, retired emperors ({{ill|lt=Daijō Tennō|Q14303973|WD=Q14303973}}) and imperial women ({{ill|lt=Nyoin|Q10075498|WD=Q10075498}}) often stopped here. In {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1373|Q6261|WD=Q6261}}), Yamaguchi Akyō, thought to have been jitō of local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamaguchi-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}A shōen from the Kamakura through Sengoku periods; the honke and ryōke are unclear. The estate is thought to have covered modern Yamaguchi-nishi in Wakayama and surrounding Kamikurodani, Fujita, Tani, Yuyadani, and Sato (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'')., donated one dan (360 tsubo, about 12 ares) from jitō stipend fields as "Rishu Zanmai rice field" for rites at the shrineCopy of "Akyō deed of land donation," dated intercalary 10th month, 7th day, same year (from "Kiketsugo-ke documents," included in "Nagusa District old documents" held by National Institute of Japanese Literature archive). Also in ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (No. Minami-211).. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1585|Q6736|WD=Q6736}}), during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}'s Kii campaign, war fire together with that affecting {{ill|lt=Negoro-ji|Q1552446|WD=Q1552446}} burned shrine buildings, treasures, and old origin records to ashes. Though many shrine fields were confiscated, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hidenaga|Q1191183|WD=Q1191183}} donated 25 koku of shrine land''Kii Zoku Fudoki''.. Rebuilding began around {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 4 (turn of 16th-17th centuries), and with aid from domain lords including the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895}} and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, as guardian deity against the demon gate of {{ill|lt=Wakayama Castle|Q1151269|WD=Q1151269}}, it became one of the finest shrines nearby. Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q2564508}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Torii and haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}Tani, Aza Hirayama 377 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|16|37.6|N|135|16|05.3|E|scale:25000}} | 祭神 = Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto | 社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 本殿 = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roof | 別名 = Hiyoshi Shrine | 札所等 = | 例祭 = October 17 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Yamaguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamaguchi Shrine''' (やまぐちじんじゃ) is a shrine in Tani, {{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}. It is located near {{ill|lt=Onoyama Pass|Q11659176|WD=Q11659176}} on the southern foot of the {{ill|lt=Izumi Mountains|Q11417784|WD=Q11417784}}, along the route from {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} to {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}. In the center of the precinct stand side-by-side '''Hiyoshi Shrine''' on the left (west) and '''Kasuga Shrine''' on the right, with Hiyoshi Shrine treated as the main shrine. Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Village Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === With '''Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (いくつひめのみこと) as principal deity, it also enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kashikone-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masakatsu Agatsu Kachihayahi Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsu-hiko Hono-ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn?}}According to ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''. ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' and ''Kii Meisho Zue'' describe central Sannō Upper Seven Shrines, Ikutsu-hime deity on the left (viewer-right), and Yateru-no-Mikoto on the right (viewer-left); ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' also lists Sannō Gongen with Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto and Kōtō Yateru-no-Mikoto. All three list {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}} and Yateru-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}), but these do not appear in the above gazette.. The exact identity of Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto is unclear, but she has long been worshipped as a deity of safe childbirth''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''.. === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was already founded by the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and during an imperial visit to Kumano by {{ill|lt=Emperor Heizei|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}, an imperial envoy enlarged the shrine buildings''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' However, no other record of Heizei's Kumano pilgrimage is known.. The surrounding area also preserves many legends of {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}}, including "Shōgun-zuka," said to be his tomb, and a site said to be the remains of the residence of Sakanoue Gorō, called his fifth-generation descendant. There is also a tradition that members of the {{ill|lt=Sakanoue clan|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}} held victory-prayer rites here''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' Yamaguchi-nishi was also called Sakanoue village and was the ancestral land of the Kii Sakanoue clan (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'').. ''{{ill|lt=Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964}}'', focusing on Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto, suggests (while noting weak evidence) that this shrine corresponds to "Ikubihime Shrine" in Nagusa District, Kii, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and to "Junior Fourth Rank Upper Ikubihime deity" in the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}; it suspects that later Buddhist clerics became {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}}, changed the deity name, and merged other deities, obscuring the original form. According to shrine tradition, because it stands on the {{ill|lt=Kumano Kaidō|Q11568976|WD=Q11568976}}, retired emperors ({{ill|lt=Daijō Tennō|Q14303973|WD=Q14303973}}) and imperial women ({{ill|lt=Nyoin|Q10075498|WD=Q10075498}}) often stopped here. In {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1373|Q6261|WD=Q6261}}), Yamaguchi Akyō, thought to have been jitō of local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamaguchi-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}A shōen from the Kamakura through Sengoku periods; the honke and ryōke are unclear. The estate is thought to have covered modern Yamaguchi-nishi in Wakayama and surrounding Kamikurodani, Fujita, Tani, Yuyadani, and Sato (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'')., donated one dan (360 tsubo, about 12 ares) from jitō stipend fields as "Rishu Zanmai rice field" for rites at the shrineCopy of "Akyō deed of land donation," dated intercalary 10th month, 7th day, same year (from "Kiketsugo-ke documents," included in "Nagusa District old documents" held by National Institute of Japanese Literature archive). Also in ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (No. Minami-211).. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1585|Q6736|WD=Q6736}}), during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}'s Kii campaign, war fire together with that affecting {{ill|lt=Negoro-ji|Q1552446|WD=Q1552446}} burned shrine buildings, treasures, and old origin records to ashes. Though many shrine fields were confiscated, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hidenaga|Q1191183|WD=Q1191183}} donated 25 koku of shrine land''Kii Zoku Fudoki''.. Rebuilding began around {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 4 (turn of 16th-17th centuries), and with aid from domain lords including the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895}} and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, as guardian deity against the demon gate of {{ill|lt=Wakayama Castle|Q1151269|WD=Q1151269}}, it became one of the finest shrines nearby. Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q2564508}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه (和歌山市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11467400}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Yamaguchi Shrine | 画像 = [[画像:Yamaguchi_Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}Tani, Aza Hirayama 377 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|16|37.6|N|135|16|05.3|E|scale:25000}} | 祭神 = Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto | 社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 本殿 = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roof | 別名 = Hiyoshi Shrine | 札所等 = | 例祭 = October 17 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Yamaguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamaguchi Shrine''' (やまぐちじんじゃ) is a shrine in Tani, {{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}. It is located near {{ill|lt=Onoyama Pass|Q11659176|WD=Q11659176}} on the southern foot of the {{ill|lt=Izumi Mountains|Q11417784|WD=Q11417784}}, along the route from {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} to {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}. In the center of the precinct stand side-by-side '''Hiyoshi Shrine''' on the left (west) and '''Kasuga Shrine''' on the right, with Hiyoshi Shrine treated as the main shrine. Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Village Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === With '''Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (いくつひめのみこと) as principal deity, it also enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kashikone-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masakatsu Agatsu Kachihayahi Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsu-hiko Hono-ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn?}}According to ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''. ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' and ''Kii Meisho Zue'' describe central Sannō Upper Seven Shrines, Ikutsu-hime deity on the left (viewer-right), and Yateru-no-Mikoto on the right (viewer-left); ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' also lists Sannō Gongen with Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto and Kōtō Yateru-no-Mikoto. All three list {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}} and Yateru-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}), but these do not appear in the above gazette.. The exact identity of Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto is unclear, but she has long been worshipped as a deity of safe childbirth''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''.. === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was already founded by the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and during an imperial visit to Kumano by {{ill|lt=Emperor Heizei|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}, an imperial envoy enlarged the shrine buildings''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' However, no other record of Heizei's Kumano pilgrimage is known.. The surrounding area also preserves many legends of {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}}, including "Shōgun-zuka," said to be his tomb, and a site said to be the remains of the residence of Sakanoue Gorō, called his fifth-generation descendant. There is also a tradition that members of the {{ill|lt=Sakanoue clan|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}} held victory-prayer rites here''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' Yamaguchi-nishi was also called Sakanoue village and was the ancestral land of the Kii Sakanoue clan (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'').. ''{{ill|lt=Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964}}'', focusing on Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto, suggests (while noting weak evidence) that this shrine corresponds to "Ikubihime Shrine" in Nagusa District, Kii, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and to "Junior Fourth Rank Upper Ikubihime deity" in the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}; it suspects that later Buddhist clerics became {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}}, changed the deity name, and merged other deities, obscuring the original form. According to shrine tradition, because it stands on the {{ill|lt=Kumano Kaidō|Q11568976|WD=Q11568976}}, retired emperors ({{ill|lt=Daijō Tennō|Q14303973|WD=Q14303973}}) and imperial women ({{ill|lt=Nyoin|Q10075498|WD=Q10075498}}) often stopped here. In {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1373|Q6261|WD=Q6261}}), Yamaguchi Akyō, thought to have been jitō of local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamaguchi-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}A shōen from the Kamakura through Sengoku periods; the honke and ryōke are unclear. The estate is thought to have covered modern Yamaguchi-nishi in Wakayama and surrounding Kamikurodani, Fujita, Tani, Yuyadani, and Sato (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'')., donated one dan (360 tsubo, about 12 ares) from jitō stipend fields as "Rishu Zanmai rice field" for rites at the shrineCopy of "Akyō deed of land donation," dated intercalary 10th month, 7th day, same year (from "Kiketsugo-ke documents," included in "Nagusa District old documents" held by National Institute of Japanese Literature archive). Also in ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (No. Minami-211).. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1585|Q6736|WD=Q6736}}), during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}'s Kii campaign, war fire together with that affecting {{ill|lt=Negoro-ji|Q1552446|WD=Q1552446}} burned shrine buildings, treasures, and old origin records to ashes. Though many shrine fields were confiscated, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hidenaga|Q1191183|WD=Q1191183}} donated 25 koku of shrine land''Kii Zoku Fudoki''.. Rebuilding began around {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 4 (turn of 16th-17th centuries), and with aid from domain lords including the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895}} and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, as guardian deity against the demon gate of {{ill|lt=Wakayama Castle|Q1151269|WD=Q1151269}}, it became one of the finest shrines nearby. Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q2564508}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Torii and haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}Tani, Aza Hirayama 377 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|16|37.6|N|135|16|05.3|E|scale:25000}} | 祭神 = Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto | 社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = Unknown | 本殿 = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roof | 別名 = Hiyoshi Shrine | 札所等 = | 例祭 = October 17 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Yamaguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamaguchi Shrine''' (やまぐちじんじゃ) is a shrine in Tani, {{ill|lt=Wakayama|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}. It is located near {{ill|lt=Onoyama Pass|Q11659176|WD=Q11659176}} on the southern foot of the {{ill|lt=Izumi Mountains|Q11417784|WD=Q11417784}}, along the route from {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} to {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}. In the center of the precinct stand side-by-side '''Hiyoshi Shrine''' on the left (west) and '''Kasuga Shrine''' on the right, with Hiyoshi Shrine treated as the main shrine. Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Village Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === With '''Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (いくつひめのみこと) as principal deity, it also enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kashikone-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masakatsu Agatsu Kachihayahi Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsu-hiko Hono-ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn?}}According to ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''. ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' and ''Kii Meisho Zue'' describe central Sannō Upper Seven Shrines, Ikutsu-hime deity on the left (viewer-right), and Yateru-no-Mikoto on the right (viewer-left); ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' also lists Sannō Gongen with Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto and Kōtō Yateru-no-Mikoto. All three list {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054}} and Yateru-no-Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}), but these do not appear in the above gazette.. The exact identity of Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto is unclear, but she has long been worshipped as a deity of safe childbirth''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette''.. === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was already founded by the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and during an imperial visit to Kumano by {{ill|lt=Emperor Heizei|Q353558|WD=Q353558}}, an imperial envoy enlarged the shrine buildings''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' However, no other record of Heizei's Kumano pilgrimage is known.. The surrounding area also preserves many legends of {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979|WD=Q941979}}, including "Shōgun-zuka," said to be his tomb, and a site said to be the remains of the residence of Sakanoue Gorō, called his fifth-generation descendant. There is also a tradition that members of the {{ill|lt=Sakanoue clan|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}} held victory-prayer rites here''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette.'' Yamaguchi-nishi was also called Sakanoue village and was the ancestral land of the Kii Sakanoue clan (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'').. ''{{ill|lt=Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964}}'', focusing on Ikutsu-hime-no-Mikoto, suggests (while noting weak evidence) that this shrine corresponds to "Ikubihime Shrine" in Nagusa District, Kii, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and to "Junior Fourth Rank Upper Ikubihime deity" in the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}; it suspects that later Buddhist clerics became {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}}, changed the deity name, and merged other deities, obscuring the original form. According to shrine tradition, because it stands on the {{ill|lt=Kumano Kaidō|Q11568976|WD=Q11568976}}, retired emperors ({{ill|lt=Daijō Tennō|Q14303973|WD=Q14303973}}) and imperial women ({{ill|lt=Nyoin|Q10075498|WD=Q10075498}}) often stopped here. In {{ill|lt=Ōan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1373|Q6261|WD=Q6261}}), Yamaguchi Akyō, thought to have been jitō of local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamaguchi-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}A shōen from the Kamakura through Sengoku periods; the honke and ryōke are unclear. The estate is thought to have covered modern Yamaguchi-nishi in Wakayama and surrounding Kamikurodani, Fujita, Tani, Yuyadani, and Sato (''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary'')., donated one dan (360 tsubo, about 12 ares) from jitō stipend fields as "Rishu Zanmai rice field" for rites at the shrineCopy of "Akyō deed of land donation," dated intercalary 10th month, 7th day, same year (from "Kiketsugo-ke documents," included in "Nagusa District old documents" held by National Institute of Japanese Literature archive). Also in ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (No. Minami-211).. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1585|Q6736|WD=Q6736}}), during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}'s Kii campaign, war fire together with that affecting {{ill|lt=Negoro-ji|Q1552446|WD=Q1552446}} burned shrine buildings, treasures, and old origin records to ashes. Though many shrine fields were confiscated, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hidenaga|Q1191183|WD=Q1191183}} donated 25 koku of shrine land''Kii Zoku Fudoki''.. Rebuilding began around {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 4 (turn of 16th-17th centuries), and with aid from domain lords including the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895}} and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kishū Tokugawa clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, as guardian deity against the demon gate of {{ill|lt=Wakayama Castle|Q1151269|WD=Q1151269}}, it became one of the finest shrines nearby. Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q2564508}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Through the Edo period it held only 2 koku of shrine land, yet retained the appearance of a major shrine with dense sacred woods and solemn halls. At that time it was called "Hiyoshi Kasuga Shrine" and treated as the {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of six surrounding villages''Kii Zoku Fudoki''. The six villages correspond to the six settlements listed above for Yamaguchi-shō.. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, its name was changed to "Hiyoshi Shrine" and it was ranked village shrine; in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42), unranked shrines from nearby former {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Village (Wakayama)|Q11466903|WD=Q11466903|lt=Yamaguchi Village}} were mergedAs reference, merged shrines (from ''Wakayama Shiyō''): * Yuyadani's Ōji Gongen-sha and Shiratori Shrine. Ōji Gongen-sha is identified with {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the {{ill|lt=Kumano 99 Ōji|Q3200280|WD=Q3200280}}; Shiratori Shrine may relate to the ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' tale of a beautiful woman transformed into a white bird. * Takihata's Nakayama Ōji-sha (identified with {{ill|lt=Nakayama Ōji|Q11370042|WD=Q11370042}} of the Kumano 99 Ōji) * Nakasuji-Hinobu's Zaō Gongen and Tsuchiya Shrine * Hiraoka's Hirano Shrine, and the name was changed to "Yamaguchi Shrine." The present parish area comprises ten hamlets: Yamaguchi-nishi, Bessho, Ochiai, Fujita, Kamikurodani, Tani, Yuyadani, Hiraoka, Sato, and Nakasuji-Hinobu. === Rituals === According to shrine tradition, those who welcomed and sent off Sakanoue no Tamuramaro when he came in the {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} era to subdue bandits were called "Jūban-gashira" and held key roles in festivals, with the office inherited through the Edo period. Formerly there were also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikoshi processions|1=UNKNOWN}} and mounted processions, but these were eventually discontinued after the Tenshō war fire''Kii Meisho Zue''.. There is also an old tradition that two crows (male and female), called "reiu," always lived in the precinct and foretold fortune or disaster. In Edo times, village heads gathered each month on the first day for "Sakuhei-kō" rites and banquet; rice was offered to these sacred crows. If crops would be good they came quickly and ate, but in times of disaster they would not eat, and people judged auspiciousness from this. Worshippers also offered food to the crows and judged whether prayers would be answered by whether they ate; these offerings were called "Tori no Meshi"''Nanki Jinja Roku''.. === Shrine priests === In the early modern period, service was performed by the '''Ki Echigo''' family, a branch of the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko house}}. === Precinct shrines === ; Kasuga Shrine : Stands east of the main shrine and enshrines the four Kasuga deities ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q2564508}}, and Himegami). Origin unknown. There are also Nunotama Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, and Ōtoshi Shrine. === Shrine buildings === The honden of Hiyoshi Shrine is a three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with three doors; Kasuga Shrine is two-bay nagare-zukuri with two doors. Both are similarly scaled, with verandas and balustrades on three sides, with side screens and simplified rear, copper-sheet roofing, and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuogi|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}}. There are also an administrative hall and shinsensho. === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Kishu Domain (ed.), ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, 1910 * Sugihara Yasushige, ''Nanki Jinja Roku'' (in ''Shintō Taikei'' Shrine Section Vol.41 [Kii/Awaji], Shintō Taikei Editorial Committee, 1987) * Takaichi Shiyū, ''Kii Meisho Zue'' 1 (retitled facsimile), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * ''Kadokawa Place Names Dictionary of Japan'' Vol.30 (Wakayama Prefecture), {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}, 1985 ISBN 4-04-001300-X * ''Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}, 1983 ISBN 9784582490312 * ''Wakayama Shiyō'' (expanded 5th ed.), Wakayama City Hall, 1965 * ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'', Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, 1995 * ''History of Wakayama City'' Vol.4 (Ancient/Medieval Sources), Wakayama City, 1977 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Ikubihime Shrine|Q11378800|WD=Q11378800}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Kinai|Q11466716|WD=Q11466716}} * {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Hyōnai|Q11466669|WD=Q11466669}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.jp/jinja-search/pdf/01018.pdf Yamaguchi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, PDF ''Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'') {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまくちしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yamaguchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
